result:
I keep thinking about the space between two heartbeats. Not the silence—there’s no silence—but the low, tidal hush that feels like the whole world is inhaling. I used to believe that if I could wedge my fingernails into that gap, I could pry it open like a trapdoor and slip through, out of the skin I’d outgrown, into a version of me who knew how to answer the phone without rehearsing failure first.

Tonight the air smells of scorched cinnamon because the neighbors’ Christmas wreath caught fire on their porch light. Orange sparks drifted over the hedge like mutant fireflies while I stood barefoot on the cold bricks, watching strangers clap the flames out with a damp bath towel printed with faded flamingos. One ember landed on the back of my wrist and left a tiny constellation: a red dot, a white halo, a reminder that damage can be smaller than a freckle and still change the orbit of your thoughts.

Inside, the kitchen clock is broken again. It keeps perfect time, but backwards, so every minute peels off the future and sticks it to the past. I’ve been living in reverse since October—coffee getting hotter as it cools, unread books fattening with new paragraphs, a voicemail from my mother un-erasing itself: “I found your old crayon box under the stairs; the one called ‘Skin’ is still unused.” I should answer, but my voice sounds like someone else’s when I hear it back, like I’ve been dubbed poorly into my own life.

I tried to draw myself tonight. Pencil, crooked mirror, 2 a.m. The line of the jaw kept sliding south; the eyes refused to match. I gave the paper my name anyway, folded it into a paper crane, and floated it in the toilet bowl, where it unfolded like a murder scene. Flushed twice; it lingered, soggy, accusing. Maybe that’s the real portrait: wet graphite bleeding into water, a face dissolving until the drain gulps and forgets.

Across town, the all-night laundromat glows like a spaceship that crash-landed in despair. Rows of metal carcasses tremble with someone else’s pockets: receipts, lipstick smears, a single bullet that clinks against the glass with every revolution, sounding like a tiny jailer rattling keys. I go there when the house is too full of yesterday’s breath. Feed quarters into the brightest machine, climb inside the drum, and hug my knees while it spins. Centrifugal force is the closest I get to forgiveness: everything heavy pressed against the ribcage until it feels like belonging. When the cycle ends I emerge dizzy, smelling of lavender and static, pockets inside-out, lint in my hair like confetti from a party I wasn’t invited to.

A man there once told me time is a kidney: it filters what matters from what merely passes through. He wore two watches, both broken, and carried a Ziploc of dried seaweed he claimed were his grandmother’s love letters translated into ocean. He offered me a strand; it tasted of salt, old ink, and the moment before a wave remembers it’s part of the sea. I chewed until my tongue stung, thinking: if I swallow enough estranged words, maybe I’ll grow gills, slip downstream, become the version of me who never learned to dread the mirror.

But I stay landlocked, ankle-deep in carpet seams, counting the ways light can fail: bulb flicker, sunrise delayed by cloud cover, the dullness behind my retinas when someone says “it gets better” like they’re reading the forecast for a country I’ll never visit. Still, I keep a list of small mercies on the fridge under a magnet shaped like a tardigrade. Today’s entries: the way the toaster smells slightly of snow, the neighbor kid who waved through the letterbox, the fact that my heart—despite rumors—continues to practice its stubborn, off-rhythm samba.

Some nights I open all the windows even in winter, let the cold comb through the rooms like a detective looking for clues. The curtains lift, ghosts of fabric, and I speak aloud the names I’ve never tasted: Aza, Solene, Lark. Names I would have given future children if the future had cooperated. The wind carries them off the sill, down the street, perhaps to the laundromat where they’ll lodge between washed shirts and be worn by strangers who feel suddenly braver. I like thinking of those syllables traveling the world stamped on someone else’s chest, tiny passports without photos.

I keep, in a shoebox, every birthday candle I ever blew out. Ninety-four nubs of wax, some still bearing the memory of a wish I can’t recall. Tonight I’ll melt them together on the stove, pour the slurry into a single new candle, taller than me. I’ll plant it upright in the backyard under the dying apple tree, light it, and walk away. Let the flame burn down for days, weeks, until winter ends or the tree decides to bloom again—whichever comes first. Maybe the molten wax will harden into a staircase I can climb out of the seasons I’ve repeated by mistake. Maybe it’ll just attract moths who think the moon fell to earth. Either way, I won’t wait to see. I’ll be inside, between two heartbeats, rehearsing the first sentence I’ll say when the phone finally rings and I recognize the voice as my own, only older, only kinder, only asking: “Tell me what color the sky is where you are.”

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
